---
title: "vellumverse #12: Millions of points, honestly"
author:
- name: David Schoch
  orcid: 0000-0003-2952-4812
date: '2026-08-06'
categories:
- R
- performance
- vellumverse
---

Two things make plotting slow at scale: solving the layout, and drawing one
object per data point. The last few posts covered the first, where the engine
moved layout into Rust and solves it once. This post is about the second, which is
a different problem. No engine speed saves you if the plot asks the renderer to
draw two million individual circles. The SVG alone would be enormous, and most of
those marks would land on the same pixel anyway.

When you have more points than the screen has pixels, drawing each one is not just
slow, it is dishonest. The dense regions saturate into a solid blob and the
structure you meant to show vanishes under the overplotting. The fix is to stop
drawing points and start drawing density.

`mark_datashade()` bins the points into a grid the size of the output image,
counts how many land in each cell, and colours the cells by that count. It returns
one raster, not millions of marks. The binning is a single pass in Rust, so the
cost scales with the number of pixels, not the number of points. Ten thousand
points and ten million cost about the same to draw, because the output is the
same-sized image either way.

```{r}
#| eval: false
library(vellumverse)
set.seed(1)
n <- 1e6
df <- data.frame(x = rnorm(n), y = rnorm(n) * 0.5 + rnorm(n))

vplot(df) |>
  mark_datashade(x = x, y = y)
```

A million points, drawn as a density field in constant time. The knob that most
changes what you see is `how`, which maps cell counts to colour. The default
`"eq_hist"` equalises the histogram so a few very dense cells do not wash out
everything else; `"log"` and `"cbrt"` are gentler compressions, and `"linear"`
uses raw counts. `colors` is the ramp from sparse to dense.

The example I keep coming back to is in the gallery: 5.2 million NBA field-goal
attempts, one facet per season from 2000 to 2025. Plotted as dots it is a
saturated smear. Datashaded, with `facet_wrap()` running the aggregation per
season and `coord_fixed()` holding each court to true proportions, the story is
unmistakable. The mid-range cools to almost nothing over two decades, the rim
stays hot, and a bright ring forms along the three-point arc. That is the Rust
backend from the fourth post earning its keep: five million points is not five
million objects, it is one binning pass and one raster per panel.

There is a real trade to name. A datashaded image is a single raster, so its
points are no longer individually addressable. You give up the per-element keys
that made the last post's tooltips work. That is the right trade above the pixel
threshold, where the individual points were invisible anyway, and the wrong one
below it, where `mark_point()` keeps every point hoverable. The rule of thumb is
just the pixel count: if the scatter has more points than the plot has pixels,
datashade; if not, draw them.

One more interaction bites people, so it is worth stating. Output format and data
size are linked. A raster PNG is a fixed grid of pixels no matter how much data
went in. A vector SVG or PDF stores every mark as an element, so a
half-million-point scatter becomes a half-million-element file. For large data,
render to raster, or datashade first so that even the vector output is a single
embedded image.

That is the last of the machinery. One post remains, the practical send-off: how
to translate what you already know from `ggplot2`, and how to teach the ecosystem
about a plot type of your own.
